Every student question was an arena for my growth
Reality check at my first frontend job: ideal vs reality
Through a series of coincidences, I went to interview at the company founded by the teacher whose course I'd taken. The position was: "Frontend Intern".
Yes, you read that right — intern.
By my age, taking an internship seemed unusual, but my mindset was:
"Age isn't the issue — shamelessness is invincible!"
How smooth was the interview process? Hmm... I've forgotten XD.
But I remember the teacher asked me JavaScript questions —
(article in Mandarin), etc. I didn't answer fluently, but I could clearly express what I understood.
Quick confession: when I started as an intern, I set myself a deadline — 6 months in, then leave to find a full-time job.
Unexpectedly, two months before I planned to resign, the teacher suddenly said:
"Ray, our company is opening a frontend engineer position. Interested?"
In that moment, the plot completely flipped — I wasn't leaving; I was being "promoted" to full-time.
Result? I've been there N years now XD.
After joining full-time, the work didn't change much — still primarily frontend dev.

But my skill tree started to drift —
Internal project dev ✔️
Course operations assistance ✔️
Part-time course TA, answering student questions ✔️
— wildly varied, from beginner to advanced, even people bringing Python at me.
Every day replying till I doubted reality — my daily life was struggling to survive the endless sea of questions~
Python is a high-level programming language mainly used for backend, data analysis, AI, etc. — different from JavaScript for frontend.
After a long period of crazy course replies, I eventually wrote an article called "
Let's Talk About Questions That Really Deserve a Roasting
" — full of my bloody hands-on reply experience. The point: how to ask questions to get faster, more accurate answers.
If there are questions that left deep impressions, they're probably:
Why does CSS behave differently across browsers?
How should I practice JavaScript (or slicing)?
After learning Vue.js, why can't I still find a frontend job?
My girlfriend wants to break up with me — what should I do?
" — completely philosophical territory. Basically everything you can think of, I've encountered XD.
To answer these, I had to research and think. So later I realized — TA skill +1: from programming Debug to relationship counseling.
Early on, browser compatibility was a major challenge for frontend devs. Different browsers had different CSS support — IE, Firefox, Chrome, Safari — each had its own quirks and limits.
differed too — the same CSS code might display differently in different browsers.
IE is in the grave now~
But we still need to handle browser default styles. How?
In dev flow, before writing CSS, we usually use a technique called
to resolve and unify browser defaults — so our CSS displays more consistently across browsers.
CSS Reset is a chunk of CSS styles that resets browser defaults — lets us start CSS from a clean state. Well-known CSS Resets:
by Eric Meyer — earliest and most famous CSS Reset. Downside: resets all default styles, so you have to re-define a lot.
an extension of Eric Meyer's Reset CSS that adds support for HTML5 tags (article, section, header), better suited for modern web dev.
a more modern CSS Reset that aligns browser defaults more closely while keeping some useful defaults.
For more on Reset CSS vs Normalize.css, see
Many frontend frameworks (Bootstrap, Foundation) include CSS Reset, so you don't need to add it separately.
Beyond unifying defaults, how do we know which CSS features are supported by which browsers?
— looks up support for various CSS properties across browsers, including detailed info: which versions support, which don't, fallback options, etc.
Even better, it can also look up JavaScript feature support.
In real dev, we don't immediately use new syntax — we check browser support first to ensure most users can use it.

So before using new syntax, remember:
"Hey browser, do you support this?"
I think no matter what tech you're learning, the key is
— write out what you learned, build something. That's how you really understand and master.
I've seen many cases stuck in the
phase — watching tons of tutorials, absorbing knowledge, but lacking practice and application.
Result: knowledge stays in short-term memory, quickly forgotten.
What are the output methods? Honestly, lots:
turn what you learned into articles. Whether or not someone has written about it, use words to test whether you really understand.
pick a topic that interests you, force yourself to solve problems through hands-on work.
try answering others' questions, explaining simply — deepens your understanding.
review others' code. You'll notice many details and different styles — improves your own code quality.
There are many practice methods, but the real key is — find what fits you and keep at it consistently. That's how you master tech.
language-txt shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
But I think there's one more key for faster growth:
Reflection isn't self-denial — it's letting yourself slowly evolve through the process.
This question is actually complex with many possibilities. I'll list the most common:

interviewers don't just look at tech — they care about your speech, attitude, and communication. After joining, you'll communicate requirements, discuss solutions, collaborate. Remember: don't just answer one-line responses without soul.
portfolios don't need flashy tech, but they must clearly convey: what you learned, what problems you solved, why you picked this tech stack. That's how interviewers see your value.

resume is the first impression, your ticket in. Information ordering, content presentation, format design — all affect interviewer impression. Don't write like a logbook: "graduated college → military → looking for work" — interviewer's eyes are rolling halfway through.

don't think learning Vue.js makes you invincible. The moment React or Node.js comes up, you crash. Tech keeps evolving, new tools emerge, requirements shift. Engineers usually need diverse skill stacks to face different challenges.
"What do you specifically value during interviews?" you might wonder.
Sharing how I run mock interviews when helping others:
I keep the interview relaxed, more like a conversation. Don't scare talent away with stiffness.
ask a few basics to confirm the resume isn't fluff.
for Sr. positions, have you slice a page or write a Todo List to verify real chops, plus give feedback for improvement.
ask soul-probing questions to check communication, teamwork, stress tolerance — soft skills.
I really value "Do you have any questions for me?" — no questions? Then they may not care, or didn't do homework.
You'll notice tech questions aren't what I value most. Tech can be trained on the job based on project needs.
are different — harder to train. Companies need:
These traits keep teams running smoothly.
Otherwise, just hiring someone technically strong but uncollaborative is at best a "

Questions aren't obstacles — they're leverage. Every student reply was a chance to fix CSS compatibility, practice JS output, build soft skills, and improve interview readiness. Ideals and reality align in the arena.
